questions.question_text: A listing firm agrees to share compensation with a buyer's firm. Under Washington law, is this permitted?
distractors: No, compensation sharing is prohibited
Only with prior DOL approval needed
Only for commercial transactions only
questions.explanation: Effective January 1, 2024, SB 5191 explicitly permits firm-to-firm compensation sharing in Washington. This allows listing firms and buyer's firms to share commissions as agreed between the parties.

questions.question_text: A seller tells their broker they want to pay a 4% commission. The broker insists the standard rate is 6%. Is this correct?
distractors: Yes, 6% is the required minimum
Yes, state law sets the rate
Only the MLS sets commission rates
questions.explanation: Commission rates in Washington are always negotiable between the parties. There is no standard or required rate. A broker who insists on a fixed rate may be violating antitrust laws and fair dealing obligations.

questions.question_text: A broker is compensated by a third party rather than by the buyer or seller. What must the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose it to all transaction parties
distractors: Refuse the additional compensation
Report the payment to the DOL
Deposit it in the firm's trust account
questions.explanation: Brokers must disclose compensation terms to all parties regardless of the source — whether from the seller, buyer, or a third party. Transparency about compensation is a core duty under Washington brokerage law.

questions.question_text: Where must the terms of a broker's compensation be documented in Washington?
distractors: In a verbal side agreement
On the MLS listing only
In the closing statement only
questions.explanation: Compensation terms must be documented in a brokerage service agreement. This written agreement ensures all parties understand the compensation structure before services are rendered.

questions.question_text: Which legislation authorized firm-to-firm compensation sharing in Washington effective January 1, 2024?
questions.explanation: Senate Bill 5191, effective January 1, 2024, explicitly permits firm-to-firm compensation sharing in Washington real estate transactions. This codified a practice that had been common but previously lacked clear statutory authority.

questions.question_text: A broker creates a social media ad for a listing. Where must the firm's licensed name appear?
questions.correct_answer: Within one click of the ad
distractors: Only on the broker's website
Nowhere if the ad is under 100 words
Only on print advertisements
questions.explanation: Under WAC 308-124B-210, social media advertisements must display the firm's and broker's licensed names no more than one click away. This ensures consumers can identify the brokerage firm behind any online advertising.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to advertise under the name 'Pacific Realty Group LLC.' What issue does this create?
questions.correct_answer: It suggests a separate entity from the firm
distractors: The name is too long for advertising
LLC names are never permitted in WA
Only managing brokers may advertise
questions.explanation: Washington prohibits brokers from using titles that suggest a separate entity, such as Inc., LLC, Corp., 'realty,' or 'company.' These terms may mislead consumers into thinking the broker operates independently from the licensed brokerage firm.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to use a personal brand name that is not their licensed name. What approval is needed?
questions.correct_answer: Written approval from the designated broker
distractors: DOL must approve the brand name
No approval is needed for branding
The managing broker gives verbal consent
questions.explanation: Brokers must get advance written approval from the designated broker before using any unlicensed title or brand name in advertising. This ensures the designated broker maintains control over how the firm is represented to the public.

questions.question_text: A broker publishes a newspaper ad listing another firm's property without permission. What rule has been violated?
distractors: The ad exceeds the character word limit
Newspaper ads are now prohibited entirely
Only managing brokers may place listing ads
questions.explanation: Washington advertising regulations prohibit brokers from advertising another broker's or firm's listing without authorization. Doing so misrepresents the broker's role and can mislead consumers about who controls the listing.

questions.question_text: A broker posts a property listing on social media without including the brokerage firm's name anywhere. What rule has been violated?
questions.correct_answer: The firm name must appear in all advertising
distractors: Social media posts are exempt from ad rules
Only the broker's name is required
Firm names are required only in print ads
questions.explanation: Washington advertising regulations require the firm's licensed name to appear clearly and conspicuously in all advertising, including social media. Online ads must display the firm and broker names within one click.

questions.question_text: A broker drafts a purchase and sale agreement using a standardized form. Is this considered practicing law?
questions.correct_answer: No, preparing standardized forms is permitted
distractors: Yes, only attorneys may draft contracts
Only designated brokers may prepare forms
Only if the broker has a law degree
questions.explanation: Washington brokers may prepare standardized forms as part of their brokerage services. This is not considered the unauthorized practice of law. However, brokers cannot draft custom legal documents or provide legal advice.

questions.question_text: After both parties sign a purchase agreement, what must the broker do with copies of the document?
questions.correct_answer: Provide copies to all signing parties
distractors: File all copies with the DOL office
Retain copies for the firm records only
Shred all copies after 30 calendar days
questions.explanation: Washington law requires brokers to provide copies of all signed documents to each signing party. This ensures all parties have records of the agreements they have entered into.

questions.question_text: A broker closes a transaction and wonders how long to keep the file. What standard applies?
questions.correct_answer: Per the firm's record retention policy
distractors: Records may be discarded at closing
State law requires exactly 3 years
Only digital records need retention
questions.explanation: Brokers must retain transaction records per their firm's record retention policy. The designated broker establishes these policies, and all affiliated licensees must comply with the firm's documentation requirements.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks their broker to draft a custom addendum addressing unique property conditions. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Recommend the buyer consult an attorney
distractors: Draft the addendum themselves
Refuse to assist with the transaction
Ask the managing broker to draft it
questions.explanation: While brokers may prepare standardized forms, drafting custom legal documents such as unique addenda constitutes the practice of law. The broker should recommend the buyer consult an attorney for custom contract language.

questions.question_text: A seller signs a listing agreement but the broker fails to give the seller a copy at signing. What obligation was violated?
questions.correct_answer: Duty to provide copies to signing parties
distractors: The duty to advertise the listed property
The duty to reconcile trust accounts
The duty to disclose all material facts
questions.explanation: Brokers must provide copies of all documents to the signing parties at the time of signing. Failing to give the seller a copy of the listing agreement violates this document handling requirement.
